coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from pumpkin for the sensing of nifuratel and temperature
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Zhifeng
Li, Yanan
Li, Jinghong
Zhang, Ziyue
Yang, Tian
Yang, Shuying
coredata.dc:description: Herein, nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal strategy. The raw materials for the preparation of N-CQDs were sourced from pumpkin and melamine. The N-CQDs suggested fascinating water solubility, favorable UV and salt resistance stability. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CQDs was carried out to be 16.7 %. The prepared N-CQDs suggested good optical features and favorable blue fluorescence under a UV lamp (365 nm). The as-prepared N-CQDs could be employed as rapid, sensitive and promising fluorescence nanoprobes to detect nifuratel because of static quenching and inter filter effect. For nifuratel detection, the linear range of 0.5–100 μM and detection limit of 0.074 μM were obtained. Furthermore, N-CQDs were subsequently applied to determine nifuratel in river water and Yili milk samples with acceptable experiment results. Significantly, N-CQDs suggested evident temperature-sensitive characteristics and were employed as fluorescent temperature sensing nanoprobes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pumpkin
Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Nifuratel
Temperature nanoprobe

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection mechanism of N-CQDs to nifuratel
Sensing of nifuratel in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the College Students\u2019 Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2438) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CQDs (inset: particle size distribution map); (B) XPS spectrum of C1s; (C) XPS spectrum of N1s; (D) XPS spectrum of O1s.

(A) UV–vis absorption (black line), excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of N-CQDs (inset: picture of N-CQDs solution under sunlight (left) and UV light (right)); (B) Emission spectra of N-CQDs under various excitation wavelength.

(A)Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of nifuratel (100 μM); (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs, nifuratel, N-CQDs + nifuratel (experimentally) and N-CQDs + nifuratel (theoretically); (C) Linear relationship of F0/F and nifuratel concentration in the range of 0.5–40 μM; (D) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of nifuratel; (E) UV–vis absorption spectrum of nifuratel (black line), excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of N-CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs with addition of different nifuratel concentration (changing from 0.5 to 100 μM); (B) Linear relationship of ln(F0/F) and nifuratel concentration in the range of 0.5–100 μM.
Schematic illustrations of the preparation of N-CQDs and for nifuratel and temperature determination.
The nifuratel measurement in real samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from pumpkin for the sensing of nifuratel and temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619 PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal strategy. The raw materials for the preparation of N-CQDs were sourced from pumpkin and melamine. The N-CQDs suggested fascinating water solubility, favorable UV and salt resistance stability. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CQDs was carried out to be 16.7 %. The prepared N-CQDs suggested good optical features and favorable blue fluorescence under a UV lamp (365 nm). The as-prepared N-CQDs could be employed as rapid, sensitive and promising fluorescence nanoprobes to detect nifuratel because of static quenching and inter filter effect. For nifuratel detection, the linear range of 0.5–100 μM and detection limit of 0.074 μM were obtained. Furthermore, N-CQDs were subsequently applied to determine nifuratel in river water and Yili milk samples with acceptable experiment results. Significantly, N-CQDs suggested evident temperature-sensitive characteristics and were employed as fluorescent temperature sensing nanoprobes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nifuratel, 5-[(methylthio) methyl]-3-[(5-nitro-2-furylmethyl) amino]-2-oxazolidinone, is a nitrofuran derivative and broad-spectrum antibiotic [1,2]. Nifuratel is mainly used for bacterial vaginosis, trichomonas vaginitis, candidal vaginitis, vulvitis, urinary system infection, digestive tract amoebiasis and Giardia disease. Alcoholic beverages should not be consumed during the use of nifuratel [3,4]. We should pay attention to monitoring adverse drug reactions during the use of nifuratel. Any adverse situation requires timely communication with a doctor [5]. Up to now, only high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) has been applied to detect nifuratel [6,7]. However, HPLC possesses many disadvantages, such as costly apparatus, complicated operational procedures, professional personnel and time-consuming [8–11]. Therefore, it was essential to establish a facile, trustworthy, not time-consuming, low-budget and alternative strategy to detect nifuratel with excellent selectivity and sensitivity.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as a new-type nanomaterial, have attracted extensive interest due to significant features, such as facile preparation strategy, excellent water-solubility, prominent photostability and non-toxicity [12–15]. These advantages were beneficial for the application of CDs in many fields, including chemical sensing, biosensing, bioimaging, phototherapy, drug carriers, catalysis and fingerprint recognition [16,17]. Some methods have been applied to develop the CDs, such as arc discharge method, laser etching method, electrochemical method, chemical oxidation method, combustion method, hydrothermal method, microwave method, ultrasonic method, template method and reverse glue beam method [18–21]. Doping is an effective method for regulating the physicochemical properties of CDs, which has received increasing attention in recent years. In order to improve the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs, tune light waves, or endow CDs with more functions and applications, heteroatoms are often embedded into the structure of CDs during the preparation process such as organic precursors and metal ions [22–25]. Due to the overlapping atomic orbitals of heteroatoms and carbon atoms, as well as the push/pull electron effects of heteroatoms, the electronic structure and chemical composition of CDs can undergo changes. Among them, nitrogen doping is one of the most commonly used effective methods to enhance the photoluminescence performance of CDs and promote the red shift of emission wavelength [26,27]. Nitrogen is adjacent to carbon and their atomic radii are similar. It can partially replace carbon and embed into the carbon skeleton, thereby affecting its internal electronic environment, changing the electronic state of CDs, adjusting the band structure, increasing the probability of radiative transitions, and ultimately improving the quantum yield of CDs [28,29]. For example, Elizabeth et al. prepared carbon quantum dot containing nitrogen with green fluorescence and high quantum yield (17.1 %). This CDs were applied for selective and sensitive determination of ferric ions from water with low detection limit of 1.32 μM [30]. Zhang et al. developed nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) through using alanine and diammonium as precursors. The NPCDs suggested excellent water solubility, high dispersibility (2.77 nm) and quantum yield (17.2 %), and could be applied to detect doxorubicin with linear range of 0.5–6.5 μM and detection limit of 12 nM [31]. Guo et al. [32] synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with a high quantum yield of 84.79 %. The NCQDs were used for the sensing of ellagic acid with wide linear range of 0.01–50 μM in view of inner filter effect. To our knowledge, melamine, a triazine nitrogen-containing heterocyclic organic compound, is rich in nitrogen elements. It can be used as a nitrogen source to functionalize the CDs. Meanwhile, melamine can enhance the photoluminescence performance of CDs.

In this paper, we employed a hydrothermal strategy to develop the nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) by using pumpkin and melamine as the carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. The N-CQDs suggested fascinating water solubility, favorable UV and salt resistance stability. The as-prepared N-CQDs were developed as potential fluorescence nanosensors for the determination of nifuratel in actual samples in view of the interaction between N-CQDs and nifuratel. Moreover, the satisfied recovery and RSD for nifuratel were obtained via a series of corresponding tests in river water and Yili milk samples. Significantly, the N-CQDs suggested evident temperature-sensitive characteristics and were employed as fluorescence temperature sensing nanoprobes (Scheme 1 ).

Sodium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium chloride, sodium carbonate, sodium oxalate, sodium sulfate, citric acid, hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, histidine, aspartic acid, glycine, arginine, proline, glucose, glutathione, melamine, enrofloxacin, metronidazole, levofloxacin, ciprofloxacin and nifuratel were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The pumpkin was obtained from food market. All materials were analytical grade without any purification. The solutions were prepared via using ultrapure water.

A UV-4500 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was employed to investigate the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of N-CQDs in a 1 × 1 cm UV cuvette. The scanning speed and range were set as 1200 nm/mir and 200–800 nm, respectively. The fluorescence spectra and intensities of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + nifuratel mixture were studied via an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) at a voltage of 400 V. The excitation and emission slits were set as 10 nm and 10 nm with a scanning speed of 1200 nm/mir. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + nifuratel mixture were recorded on a FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France). The morphology and particle size of N-CQDs were investigated by using a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. A VG Multilab 2000X X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Electron, USA) was employed to study the element valence state. The functional group types on the surface of N-CQDs were investigated via a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Electron, USA). The pH value of solution was adjusted via a pH S-3C pH meter (Shanghai Scientific Instruments Company, China).

The establishment of N-CQDs was carried out by using a hydrothermal strategy in view of published literature with minor modifications [33]. The pumpkin and melamine were used as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. In detail, dried pumpkin powder of 1 g and melamine powder of 0.2 g were sequentially added into ultrapure water of 30 mL with stirring for 15 min at ordinary temperature. Next, the above solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for 5 h at 180 ℃. After cooling to room temperature, the product solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm at room temperature to remove the larger suspended particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was dialyzed via a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) for 36 h at room temperature. At last, the aqueous solution was stored in a refrigerator for further fluorescence sensing.

The determination of nifuratel was carried out at ordinary temperature. The detection system was developed via adding various nifuratel amounts into the detection system (0.1 mL CQDs solution (5 mg/mL) and 1.9 mL PBS solution (pH = 6.0)). When the mixture was thoroughly mixed for 60 s, the fluorescence emission data of sensing system were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The selectivity tests were measured by using the same strategy without adding nifuratel.

To study the feasibility of N-CQDs in real samples, an actual model was established. The river water samples were obtained from campus. The Yili milk samples were purchased from Tangjiu convenience store. To remove potential larger suspended particles, these actual samples were centrifuged at 4000 rpm at room temperature (15 min). Next, the supernatant was diluted with buffer solution. The nifuratel solution was added into these processed samples. Finally, the spiked tests were carried out via using fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CQDs was carried out according to the published references [34]. The quinine sulfate was used as a reference. The absorbance of N-CQDs and quinine sulfate were performed on the ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs and quinine sulfate were carried out by using the fluorescence spectrophotometer. The specific calculation formula was displayed as follows.
QY = Q
Y R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
where QY and QYR represent the fluorescent quantum yields of the N-CQDs and quinine sulfate; I and I R refer to the integral areas of fluorescence spectrum for the N-CQDs and quinine sulfate; A and AR represent the absorbance of the N-CQDs and quinine sulfate under the excitation wavelength of 375 nm; η and η R refer to the refractive index of the N-CQDs and quinine sulfate. The fluorescence quantum yield was 16.7 %.

From Fig. 1 A, some dispersed spherical particles could be observed from TEM image of N-CQDs. The diameter of the particles was statistically analyzed in detail, and its average particle size was 3.8 ± 0.04 nm. Next, the FT-IR and XPS characterization were applied to discuss the chemical functional groups of N-CQDs. From FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1), some characteristic peaks could be observed. A wide band in the range of 3645–3155 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl or amino group [34]. The excellent water solubility of N-CQDs was scribed to the existence of hydroxyl or amino group. The stretching vibration of C–H bond could be observed at the characteristic peaks of 2962 and 2827 cm−1. The peaks at 1388 cm−1 and 772 cm−1 were assigned to the deformation vibration of C–H and N–H bonds, respectively [35]. The stretching vibration peaks of C=O (1632 cm−1), C-N (1348 cm−1) and C-O (1118 cm−1) were observed [36]. The existence of N–H and C-N bonds indicated that nitrogen has been doped into the CQDs. Furthermore, XPS analysis was applied to further check the FT-IR results. From full XPS spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. S2), the characteristic peaks of 285.4 eV (C1s), 400.7 eV (N1s) and 533.6 eV (O1s) were found, and their percentage content were 61.72 %, 22.93 % and 24.49 %, respectively. Apparently, the nitrogen element has been doped into CQDs. In addition, the single XPS spectra of each element were sequentially discussed via peak processing. Explicitly, the single XPS spectrum of C1s in Fig. 1B was carried out to three feature peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV and 287.8 eV which were assigned to C–C/C=C, C-O/C-N and O-C=O bonds, respectively [37]. In Fig. 1C, two small peaks originated from the decomposition of single XPS spectrum of N1s. The feature peaks at 398.9 eV and 400.4 eV were ascribed to the pyridinic N and pyrrolic N bonds, respectively [38]. The C=O and C-OH/C-O-C bonds were observed at 531.3 eV and 532.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 1D) [39]. The XPS and FT-IR results were consistent with each other.

The optical characterizations of the as-prepared N-CQDs were investigated via UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was first studied. From UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2 A), a distinct characteristic peak at 276 nm was assigned to the n → π* transition of carbon–oxygen double bond on the surface of N-CQDs [40]. Furthermore, a wide characteristic peak at 453 nm was observed under the maximum excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The color of N-CQDs solution was discussed. When the N-CQDs solution was placed under sunlight and UV light, its color was faint yellow and blue, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated to be 16.7 %. The N-CQDs suggested an excitation-dependent performance (Fig. 2B). The above feature was ascribed to the change of emission wavelength originating from the variation of excitation wavelength. The excitation-dependent feature was ascribed to the quantum effect and different emissive trap sites on the surface of N-CQDs [41].

The stability of fluorescence probe was a significant factor for further applications. The fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs were investigated under various conditions, such as storage time, UV irradiation time, NaCl concentration and pH value. First of all, the N-CQDs solution was stored for 15 days at room temperature. From Fig. S3A, a negligible weakening for fluorescence intensity was observed after 15 days, implying the prominent stability of N-CQDs. Next, the N-CQDs solution was exposed to ultraviolet radiation for 5 min at room temperature. It could be distinctly seen that only 0.74 % of the intensity weakening appeared, suggesting the dominant photostability of N-CQDs (Fig. S3B). Subsequently, the effect of ionic strength on stability of N-CQDs was discussed. In Fig. S3C, there was no weakening phenomenon in fluorescence intensity. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was recorded under various pH value. When the pH value of N-CQDs changed from 4 to 11, the fluorescence intensity presented an upward convex parabolic trend (Fig. S3D). The lower intensity at excessive acidity or alkalinity was assigned to the process of protonation and deprotonation. Due to the above results, the N-CQDs solution displayed significant stability under different storage time, UV irradiation time and NaCl concentration, which was indispensable for accurate determination of target in complex situations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection mechanism of N-CQDs to nifuratel
ce:section.ce:para: To study the measurement mechanism of this platform for nifuratel, a series of characterizations were carried out under their corresponding conditions. The fluorescent spectra of N-CQDs were recorded without and with the addition of nifuratel. The experiment data were displayed in Fig. 3 A. From Fig. 3A, the blue fluorescence clearly disappeared when the nifuratel was added into N-CQDs solution. As we could see from Fig. 3A, a high quenching efficiency of 87.3 % was observed by comparing the fluorescence intensity of sensing platform before and after adding nifuratel (the nifuratel amount was 100 μM). The above test indicated that nifuratel could clearly weaken the blue fluorescence of N-CQDs. The potential quenching mechanisms of N-CQDs were attributed to the concrete interaction between nifuratel and the N-CQDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs, nifuratel and N-CQDs + nifuratel solution were suggested in Fig. 3B. As we could see from Fig. 3B, the absorbance of N-CQDs solution increased obviously in the presence of nifuratel (100 μM). The spectral shape of N-CQDs + nifuratel solution was similar to the theoretical spectrum. However, the absorbance of N-CQDs + nifuratel solution was lower than theoretical value. This result was ascribed to the possibility of existence of static quenching [42,43]. To clarify the fluorescence sensing mechanism by nifuratel, the fluorescence data were processed by using the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = Ksv[Q] + 1, F0 and F refer to the fluorescence emission intensity of sensing solution without and with the addition of nifuratel, Q stands for the nifuratel concentration, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant) [44,45]. From Fig. 3C, as the concentration of nifuratel gradually increased, the fluorescence intensity displayed a significant decrease. The Stern-Volmer equation could be applied to discuss static quenching and dynamic quenching. The difference could not be distinguished by using Stern-Volmer equation. Moreover,

the fluorescence lifetimes of sensing solution without and with the addition of nifuratel were recorded. From Fig. 3D, the fluorescence lifetimes of sensing solution without and with the addition of nifuratel were 3.84 ns and 3.72 ns, respectively. Minor differences of lifetime value implied that a groundstate interaction between N-CQDs and nifuratel was observed. This phenomenon confirmed the presence of static quenching [46,47]. In addition, the ultraviolet and fluorescence characterization were carried out. As suggested in Fig. 3E, the UV–vis absorption range of nifuratel was located at 305–425 nm, and a broad characteristic peak in fluorescence excitation spectrum was observed at the range of 300–425 nm. Apparently, a wide overlap range between UV–vis spectrum and fluorescence excitation was observed. The overlap phenomenon was assigned to the inner filter effect [48,49]. In conclusion, the fluorescence sensing principles of N-CQDs by nifuratel were ascribed to the static quenching and inner filter effect.

Before the determination of nifuratel, some tests were carried out to investigate the best measurement conditions. The pH value was an important sensing parameter. Therefore, it was essential to discuss the influence of pH value on the sensing of nifuratel. The tests were carried out under different pH value buffer solutions. From Fig. S4A, the fluorescence emission intensities of N-CQDs (diluting with different pH value buffer solutions) without and with the addition of nifuratel were recorded. The F0/F value was used to evaluate sensitivity N-CQDs for nifuratel sensing. When the pH was 6.0, the F0/F could reach a maximum value. Thus, the buffer solution of 6.0 (pH value) was selected as optimal detection condition. Moreover, the optimal determination time was carried out to be nifuratel through similar experiments (30 s).

The selectivity was momentous for the sensing of nifuratel in real samples. Therefore, the possible control substances were performed to study the selectivity of the as-developed platform. These substances included Na+, K+, Mg2+, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, C2O4 2-, citric acid, histidine, aspartic acid, glycine, arginine, proline, glucose, glutathione, enrofloxacin, metronidazole, levofloxacin and ciprofloxacin. As shown in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence of N-CQDs was quenched by nifuratel. However, the control substances hardly had a significant impact on the fluorescence of N-CQDs except for enrofloxacin, levofloxacin and ciprofloxacin. The fluorescence intensity increased in the presence of enrofloxacin, levofloxacin and ciprofloxacin, which were ascribed to their self-structure. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs system in the presence of nifuratel and control substances significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity (green bars) (Fig. S5B). Obviously, compared to nifuratel alone, the coexistence of nifuratel and control substances did not show significant changes. The above results suggested the dominant selectivity of N-CQDs as fluorescence probes for the measurement of nifuratel.

Before studying the practicality of this proposed strategy, its analytical behavior was discussed. The fluorescence emission data of N-CQDs-based sensor were obtained in the presence of various amounts of nifuratel under the optimal detection conditions. When the nifuratel was spiked into the detection system, its fluorescence intensity indicated a significant weakening phenomenon (Fig. 4 A). A linear model based on the relationship between ln(F0/F) and nifuratel amount was developed. The linear equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.02[Q] + 0.076 with a high correlation coefficient of 0.9981 (Fig. 4B). A wide linear range of 0.5–100 μM and low detection limit (LOD) of 0.074 μM were obtained via by processing relevant data. This strategy was carried out to compare with other analytical methods, and the results were displayed in Table S1. Although the linear range was not too ideal, the as-developed strategy held a series of advantages including lower LOD, simple synthesis process and significant stability. Due to the analyzing behavior of N-CQDs, it was practicable to detect nifuratel in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of nifuratel in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To carry out the dependability of this platform, the sensing of nifuratel in river water and Yili milk samples were discussed. The river water and Yili milk samples were handled according to Section 2.4. Various amounts of nifuratel were added into the river water and Yili milk samples and discussed via this developed platform. From Table 1 , the recovery rates of nifuratel in river water and Yili milk samples were carried out to be 96.4 %-102.1 % and 98.1 %-105.4 %, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSD) was less than 4.10 %, implying excellent accuracy. Thus, this strategy suggested excellent feasibility and could be employed to measure nifuratel in actual samples.

As displayed in Fig. S6A, when temperature increased from 20 ℃ to 100 ℃, its fluorescence intensity displayed a significant quenching phenomenon. A linear model based on the relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature was developed. The linear equation was F = -24.5Tem + 4277 with a high correlation coefficient of 0.9821. A wide linear range of 20–70 ℃ was obtained via by processing relevant data (Fig. S6B). Moreover, this temperature sensor could cycle multiple times, and the fluorescence intensity would not significantly decrease (Fig. S6C).

For the first time, the N-CQDs from pumpkin and melamine were swimmingly developed via a hydrothermal strategy. The as-prepared N-CQDs held fascinating water solubility, favorable UV and salt resistance stability. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CQDs was carried out to be 16.7 %. Blue fluorescence could be observed under UV lamp (365 nm). Moreover, the fluorescence of N-CQDs could be plainly weakened with increasing nifuratel amount from 0 to 100 μM. The as-synthesized N-CQDs could be employed as turn-off fluorescence nanosensors for the determination of nifuratel with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. The detection mechanisms were primarily due to the static quenching and inner filter effect. Moreover, N-CQDs were subsequently applied to determine nifuratel in river water and Yili milk samples with acceptable experiment results. Significantly, N-CQDs suggested an evident temperature-sensitive characteristic and were employed to temperature sensing nanoprobes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2438) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125758.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nifuratel reduces Salmonella survival in macrophages by extracellular and intracellular antibacterial activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Xie, G.F. Liu, J.Y. Ma, Y.N. Wang, R. Gao, S.Z. Geng, X.N. Jiao, P. Barrow, Nifuratel reduces Salmonella survival in macrophages by extracellular and intracellular antibacterial activity, Microbiol. Spectr. 11 (2023).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoparticle mediated codelivery of nifuratel and doxorubicin for synergistic anticancer therapy through STAT3 inhibition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.L. Zheng, Z.W. Chen, A.M. Cai, X.L. Lin, X.Y. Jiang, B. Zhou, J. Wang, Q. Yao, R.J. Chen, L.F. Kou, Nanoparticle mediated codelivery of nifuratel and doxorubicin for synergistic anticancer therapy through STAT3 inhibition, Colloid. Surface. B 193 (2020) 111109.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activity of nitrofurantoin and nifuratel against anaerobic gram-negative bacilli

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W Brumfitt, A.V Reynolds, J.M.T Hamilton-Miller, Activity of nitrofurantoin and nifuratel against anaerobic gram-negative bacilli, The Lancet 305 (1975) 460.
B.R. Pujari, F.R. Macis, A. Fairley, J.N. Ward-McQuaid, Nifuratel in Chronic Urinary Infections, The Journal of Urology 107 (1972) 112-116.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nifuratel-containing initial anti-helicobacter pylori triple therapy in children

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.A. Nijevitch, V.U. Sataev, E.N. Akhmadeyeva, A.G. Arsamastsev, Nifuratel-containing initial anti-helicobacter pylori triple therapy in children, Helicobacter 12(2) (2007) 132-135.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of nifuratel in human plasma by HPLC and study on its pharmacokinetics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.W. Wu, B.R. Xiang, J. Yang, J.J. Fu, J.H. Jiang, Determination of nifuratel in human plasma by HPLC and study on its pharmacokinetics, J. Chromatogr. Sci. 43 (2005) 179-182.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An improved HPLC method for determination of nifuratel in human plasma and its application to pharmacokinetics studies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.L. Li, B.Y. Li, M.Y. Ni, B.J. Wang, R.C. Guo, An improved HPLC method for determination of nifuratel in human plasma and its application to pharmacokinetics studies, Eur. J. Drug Metab. Ph. 32 (2007) 69-73.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor fluorescence strategy for rapid and simultaneous detection of antibiotics in wastewaters and the entire process detection mechanisms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.Y. Liu, Y.M. Ge, Y. Wei, D.F. Xing, N.Q. Ren, S.H. Ho, Multicolor fluorescence strategy for rapid and simultaneous detection of antibiotics in wastewaters and the entire process detection mechanisms, Sensor. Actuat. B 423 (2025) 136726.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monolayer-fluorescence counting for ultrasensitive detection of tumour cell-derived extracellular vesicles using a step-wedge microfluidic platform
